label: Gaussian Curvature
definition: A single number attached to each point of a surface that measures how the surface curves intrinsically, positive where it bows like a sphere, negative where it saddles, zero where it can be flattened, and computable from distances alone without reference to any surrounding space.

body.text: Gaussian curvature is the most important single-number measure of how a two-dimensional surface bends at a point. At any point a surface curves by different amounts in different directions; the two extreme values, the largest and smallest of these directional bendings, are the principal curvatures. Gaussian curvature is their product. Where both principal curvatures bow the same way, as on a sphere or the outside of an egg, the product is positive and the surface is locally dome-like. Where they bow opposite ways, as on a saddle or a mountain pass, one is positive and one negative, the product is negative, and the surface is saddle-shaped. Where at least one principal curvature is zero, as on a cylinder or a flat sheet, the product is zero and the surface is, in the relevant sense, flat.

The reason Gaussian curvature is celebrated rather than just convenient is Carl Friedrich Gauss's Theorema Egregium of 1827, the "remarkable theorem". Gauss proved that although he defined this quantity using the way the surface sits in three-dimensional space, the answer in fact depends only on the surface's intrinsic geometry, the distances measured within the surface itself as encoded by the metric tensor. An inhabitant confined to the surface, who can measure lengths and angles along it but can never look at it from outside, could compute the Gaussian curvature at every point. This is what makes it intrinsic curvature, and it is why a flat map of a sphere must distort distances: the sphere has positive Gaussian curvature and the plane has zero, and no bending without stretching can turn one into the other. A cylinder, by contrast, has zero Gaussian curvature, which is why a sheet of paper rolls into a tube without wrinkling but cannot wrap a ball.

Gaussian curvature governs the behavior of geodesics and triangles, tying it to the rest of differential-geometry. On a positively curved surface, initially parallel geodesics converge and the angles of a geodesic triangle sum to more than a straight angle; on a negatively curved surface they diverge and the angles sum to less. The Gauss-Bonnet theorem makes this global and exact: integrating the Gaussian curvature over a whole closed surface yields a topological constant, two times pi times the surface's Euler characteristic, independent of how the surface is bent. Local shape and global topology are bound together by curvature. For surfaces, Gaussian curvature also coincides, up to a factor, with the scalar curvature that generalizes "how curved" to a riemannian-manifold of any dimension, so it is the two-dimensional anchor for the higher-dimensional curvature machinery.

In machine learning the sign of curvature is a design decision about representation. Negatively curved (hyperbolic) space, where Gaussian curvature is everywhere negative, has volume that grows exponentially with radius, which is exactly the room that tree-like and hierarchical data need; this is the geometric basis of hyperbolic-embeddings. Positively curved spherical space is the natural home for directional data and for normalized embeddings compared by cosine similarity. Flat Euclidean space, with zero curvature, is the default that most models assume by omission. Curvature also describes the loss landscape itself, where the local geometry around a minimum, encoded by second-order information, shapes how optimization moves. The enduring lesson of Gaussian curvature, that some bending is intrinsic to a space and cannot be flattened away, is the same lesson that tells a practitioner when flat embeddings will distort their data and a curved geometry is required instead.
